attachments.fallback: <https://twitter.com/paulbiggar|@paulbiggar>: <https://twitter.com/geofflangdale|@geofflangdale> <https://twitter.com/graydon_pub|@graydon_pub> <https://twitter.com/yminsky|@yminsky> <https://twitter.com/slava_pestov|@slava_pestov> There's two ways that programming is text now, and one doesn't imply the other. One is "I keep my programs in files and edit them with my editor" and one is "my programs are written with words not symbols". You can drop the former without programming in hieroglyphics

attachments.fallback: <https://twitter.com/warianoguerra|@warianoguerra>: Dependency injection and registries are a workaround to the fact that module systems in languages are not first class, they are a big global singleton. I would like to create a clean instance and pass it as a parameter, inject it in the scope resolution chain or as root (sandbox)
attachments.text: Dependency injection and registries are a workaround to the fact that module systems in languages are not first class, they are a big global singleton. I would like to create a clean instance and pass it as a parameter, inject it in the scope resolution chain or as root (sandbox)

text: If so, what's the difference between modules and objects then? If it's basically the same, do modules have to be first class?

there's not much of a difference, but you have to treat them differently. You can't access all the implicit behaviour of modules with objects, the closest I saw was the with statement in javascript and object-capability model, but why not just make modules just objects and unify the resolution logic in scopes to allow modifying/introspecting the scope chain

in many programming languages you can't pass modules as values. Imagine if the top of your module instead of being a sequence of imperative imports and renames was treated as an interface your module expects to be instantiated, and then you can override the default references (provided by the global module system), mocking and sandboxing would be builtin

Well, such change will make whole thing more flexible. Make mocking in tests much easier.

Not sure would it make things easier though.
mocking, sandboxing, dependency injection and registries wouldn't be needed, that's a big simplification
also, module/object distinction would disappear and the dual resolution between scopes and global module system would disappear too
I would like to have to think about less things :slightly_smiling_face:
But this big singleton thing is not going away, right?
no, it's just another instance that's there by default, you can remove it explicitly or introspect it

```Newspeak is a new programming language in the tradition of Self and Smalltalk. Newspeak is highly dynamic and reflective - but designed to support modularity and security. It supports both object-oriented and functional programming.

Like Self, Newspeak is message-based; all names are dynamically bound. However, like Smalltalk, Newspeak uses classes rather than prototypes. As in Beta, classes may nest. Because class names are late bound, all classes are virtual, every class can act as a mixin, and class hierarchy inheritance falls out automatically. Top level classes are essentially self contained parametric namespaces, and serve to define component style modules, which naturally define sandboxes in an object-capability style.```
don't know a lot about the details but Newspeak sounds like it's going in that direction

Seems like so.

Don't know what to say, I still feel pessimistic about it. Things are really complicated when modules do have state.
There are two problems: dependencies of code and dependencies of state. If modules are just pack of functions without internal state, I guess it will make it easier to tackle with second problem.
I like immutable languages :wink:
modules as stateless interfaces with associated implementations
you can layer partial implementations, like scopes to override different parts
yeah, good idea
<https://youtu.be/TrNlNjwzzmc?t=5m57s>

I feel this is related to the idea of early/late binding (wrt time T). In Python you can 'overwrite' a module at runtime by doing something like
```
import moduleA
moduleA.moduleB = myModuleB
```
This will affect all references to `ModuleB.foo` from within moduleA (but not all cases, only explicit global lookups of ModuleB).
You can also pass modules around as objects. Are you thinking about something similar?

that's one part, it's kind of first class because it behaves like an object, but changes (to alter resolution of some value inside the module) are global and as you mentioned there's no way to change the values for other modules who hold a reference to moduleB (not through moduleA). What can be achieved in python sounds like monkey patching in ruby, and we know what that's like :slightly_smiling_face:

Yes its similar. You can also overwrite the global `sys.modules['moduleB'] = newModuleB` and it will change for all modules that reference moduleB (because the runtime lookup goes through that dict). Monkey patching is powerful (and make mocking etc very easy) but the issue is doing it in a controlled, transactional way.
Sorry I misspoke - changing sys.modules will not change the already bound imports.

In new versions of Python you can partially hack this by implementing __getattr__  at the top level of your module (<https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0562/>). In general I think first class modules and good support for 'routing/binding' modules at runtime seem like a good idea.

[May 30th, 2018 12:05 PM] stefanlesser: I was thinking recently that it would be great to have a list of values, sort of a manifesto, not unlike the Agile Manifesto, to at least have a discussion about the values we share, and to find out where we differ. I think a lot of research is opportunistic (and that's not a bad thing), but lacks a big picture of where we want to take this.

Ultimately, it should be something that allows developers of different platforms and languages to unite and at least discover shared values. One other thing we're incredibly good at is building all these silos of different language and platform communities, where we can all re-invent wheels all the time. I think there is a benefit in this language diversity, but it's hard to reap, because these different communities seldom talk to each other.

text: This thread has given me a few tabs to read, but if "Declarative > Imperative" is gaining push-back, I'm totally in the right place :smile:
This thread has given me a few tabs to read, but if "Declarative > Imperative" is gaining push-back, I'm totally in the right place :smile:

Hmm, I'm torn. It's possible Dave is a blub programmer [1] or a macho masochist like Robert Martin [2] (he doesn't actually say "sissy" in the post), but I find myself nodding along to this statement:

> ..what will be the limitations of your language? What can’t it do? It won’t be fast enough for certain things. You’re building an abstraction and they’re always leaky, so people will need to know what they can’t do.

Though I don't agree with this part:

> Otherwise they’ll have to learn the lower level details, which defeats the purpose of an easier language.

Knowing how an abstraction works doesn't defeat its purpose. This a classic overuse of the word "abstraction" to take on division-of-labor connotations: <http://akkartik.name/post/libraries>

But it's super important to know the limitations of the tools we use, and this is something most people fail at. The world doesn't really make it easy. We're all competing for attention by over-inflating claims of how easy something will be. Just start using me and all your problems will disappear. I wish we could return to a more innocent world where claims were more restrained. Like the Royal Society or something..

---

Independent of this defense of Dave, I actually tend to be pretty anti-abstraction myself, so this debate is welcome. One possible entry point into my belief system:

<https://lobste.rs/s/zyomiu/what_does_it_mean_design_software_well#c_yvvkwm>

[1] <http://wiki.c2.com/?BlubParadox>
[2] <http://blog.cleancoder.com/uncle-bob/2017/10/04/CodeIsNotTheAnswer.html>

text: 'Abstraction' is a broad term. Isn't the very notion of 'procedure' or 'data type' an abstraction? What if these are 'wrong abstractions'? I don't know how to think at this level without trading one abstraction for another (e.g. objects/messaging).

'Abstraction' is a broad term. Isn't the very notion of 'procedure' or 'data type' an abstraction? What if these are 'wrong abstractions'? I don't know how to think at this level without trading one abstraction for another (e.g. objects/messaging).

Yup, my post basically said stop using the term except for extracting functions or methods in your own code.
 If you can't easily jump from an interface to its implementation it's not an abstraction.

Yup, my post basically said stop using the term except for extracting functions or methods in your own code.
 If you can't easily jump from an interface to its implementation it's not an abstraction.

My rant on this is that we programmers need to replace our obsession with programming languages with an obsession for runtimes[1]. The runtime is the car, the language is the color of the bumper.

For me the big problem boils down to making it easy to copy code from one codebase to another, and have confidence when you're done that you didn't cause any regressions.

If you can do this, working with multiple runtimes becomes a lot more tractable.

(Avoiding bugs is a separate problem. As is coming up with the ideal architecture for a codebase. Both these seem about as solvable as world peace. But making codebases more malleable seems like the most ambitious problem we can work on that actually may admit a solution.)

[1] This may not be the best thing I've written on the subject, but it's something: <https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=10397026#10397931>

Hi everyone! Lately I tend to introduce myself as a barbarian programmer. I mean that in a few different ways:

• I tend to be a curmudgeon, happier by myself than "organizing" into groups to achieve some shared goal. This doesn't mean I dislike collaboration. I just think we tend to conflate collaboration with _synchronous_ collaboration, and asynchronous collaboration is a better fit for many tasks. Asynchronous collaboration can span great distances in space and time. You reading Aristotle could be asynchronous collaboration -- if you are then able to build on what you read in some way and put out some artifact that someone else can collaborate with. UX people like to say "don't make me think". I like to say "don't make me talk to you". Show me what you built in a form that I can read, listen to, play interactively with and gradually understand/appreciate. I may still come talk to you if the situation merits it (I actually love talking to people, believe it or not), but give me the choice.
• I think we've been doing city-living cheek-by-jowl for so long that we've forgotten what benefits it has, and continue cargo-culting it long after it's become superfluous. I don't mean that literally; I love living in cities. But our software stacks have many of the same patterns as cities, and they aren't really necessary: a) promiscuously adding dependencies, b) relying implicitly on some sort of division of labor, so that if we find a problem in a dependency we don't feel empowered to fix it for ourselves, instead turning to ways to try to get others to do it for us like paying, bullying, cajoling, passing laws, etc. It's worth swinging that pendulum back a bit, to imagine we're out on the steppe with just what we have on our backs and nobody else around for miles, and try to achieve our ends with 10x fewer libraries and frameworks. (More details: <https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=16882140#16882555> .)
• It emphasizes sustainability. When you live off the land (I gather; I wouldn't actually know) you are more attuned to how you affect your environment. Your _externalities_. We city dwellers just expect someone else to cart our trash out for us.

Unfortunately this is all aspirational, because the way we write software doesn't really work for a barbarian. I've been working very slowly on extricating a kernel of software and way of writing software that can be more self-sufficient and sustainable. I started out building a new programming language based on Lisp (<http://akkartik.name/post/wart>, which itself grew out of a startup; those who can't startup shave yaks), then grew frustrated with the stack below me and simultaneously realized languages don't matter, and build a toy VM and OS (<https://github.com/akkartik/mu#readme> ; <http://akkartik.name/about>), used it to teach programming for a couple of years, then grew frustrated with how slow it was and gradually faced up to the need to understand even more low-level details. Lately I've been building simple tools for programming in machine code (<https://github.com/akkartik/mu/tree/master/subx#readme>), but in the past week I've set this aside to design a type-safe language that I can bootstrap out of machine code (no link yet).

attachments.title: akkartik/mu
attachments.text: Soul of a tiny new machine. Track side effects in safe Assembly → More thorough tests → More malleable and rewrite-friendly software. - akkartik/mu

text:
This is my umpteenth attempt at trying to set out my dissatisfactions and motivations, so if it made no sense but you're somehow still reading, maybe one of my earlier attempts will help triangulate (in reverse chronological order):

<https://lobste.rs/s/mdmcdi/little_languages_by_jon_bentley_1986#c_3miuf2>
<https://www.reddit.com/r/ProgrammingLanguages/comments/8i33hq/mu_revisited>
<https://plforums.org/practice/minimal-features-required-for-an-extensible-language#post_65>
<https://lobste.rs/s/n0d3qo#c_rue8pf>
<http://akkartik.name/about>

I get the sense a lot of you are concerned with more high-level concerns like high level languages, live programming, Bret Victor, visual programming and so on. Think of me as trying to build better foundations for your castles. Because the crap you're building on today, it ain't gonna last. (It won't just disintegrate one day, instead it'll gradually put out shoots and suffocate all your degrees of freedom until you're totally ossified.)

(I highly recommend <https://www.ribbonfarm.com/2011/03/10/the-return-of-the-barbarian>, which radically shifted my sense of what a barbarian is, and how they've historically interacted with sedentary peoples. Also worth reading is <http://akkartik.name/post/lemmings> . The book it's from is one of ten hardcopy books I own, and I wrote the Wikipedia page for it: <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fates_of_Nations> (History is an enduring interest. "History seems to me so important that it's misleading to treat it as a mere field of study. Another way to describe it is _all the data we have so far_." -- <http://paulgraham.com/essay.html> ))

(I also like parentheses. (I've done some Lisp programming.))

attachments.title: The Return of the Barbarian
attachments.text: Our cartoon view of history goes straight from the Flintstones to Jetsons without developmental stages of any consequence in between. Hunter-gatherers and settled modern civilizations loom large, a…
attachments.fallback: ribbonfarm: The Return of the Barbarian

attachments.title: Fates of Nations
attachments.text: The Fates of Nations: A Biological Theory of History is a 1980 book by Paul Colinvaux, professor of ecology at Ohio State University.
